ETFdata <- read.table("./MarketDataUnit1.csv",
                      header = TRUE,
                      sep = ",",
                      dec = ".")
#downloading the data table for the further manipulations
ETFdata$Date <- as.Date(ETFdata$Date) #Here we set the data in appropriate format for date-Axis in the further plots

Part a

summary(ETFdata[,!(names(ETFdata) %in% "Date")]) #summary on the indices data without date column in it
##       ATX            DAX         Eurostoxx50   Nasdaq.Composite    Hangseng    
##  Min.   :2055   Min.   :11556   Min.   :2958   Min.   :10466    Min.   :14687  
##  1st Qu.:3154   1st Qu.:14397   1st Qu.:3903   1st Qu.:12335    1st Qu.:18382  
##  Median :3389   Median :15629   Median :4218   Median :14226    Median :21134  
##  Mean   :3393   Mean   :16539   Mean   :4301   Mean   :14724    Mean   :21658  
##  3rd Qu.:3661   3rd Qu.:18492   3rd Qu.:4878   3rd Qu.:16735    3rd Qu.:24107  
##  Max.   :4614   Max.   :24065   Max.   :5464   Max.   :21456    Max.   :29152

Part b

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata, aes(x = Date, y = ATX)) +
  geom_line(color = "coral1")+
  theme_grey()

# Plot for ATX
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata, aes(x = Date, y = DAX)) +
  geom_line(color = "goldenrod3")+
  theme_grey()

# Plot for DAX
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata, aes(x = Date, y = Eurostoxx50)) +
  geom_line(color = "aquamarine3")+
  theme_grey()

# Plot for Eurostoxx50
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata, aes(x = Date, y = Nasdaq.Composite)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkorchid")+
  theme_grey()

# Plot for NASDAQ composite
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata, aes(x = Date, y = Hangseng)) +
  geom_line(color = "lightblue3")+
  theme_grey()

## Plot for Hangseng

Part c

library(tidyr)
ETFdatalong <- ETFdata %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(ATX, DAX, Eurostoxx50, Nasdaq.Composite, Hangseng),
    names_to = "Index",
    values_to = "Prices"
  )
# We compound the data table into the long format so the gathering plot can be made easier
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdatalong, aes(x = Date, y = Prices, color = Index))+
  geom_line()+
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Closing prices",
       title = "Historic closing prices of 5 ETFs",
       subtitle = "around the world",
       )+
  theme_dark()

# plot for the closing prices of 5 ETFs

So, the plot of all the ETFs combined offers us an appropriate picture of the price developments accordingly to the legend. Basically, ATX and Eurostoxx50 can be characterized as more or less robust indices without large volatility risks, as for example such of the Hangseng ETF. On the other hand, DAX, NASDAQ composite and Hangseng show a higher growth rate in comparison to both other indices, which can be a useful observation for some investors. Due to this feature, risk-seeking investors might find such securities more attractive. As a result, they prefer these rather than ATX, for example, which shows considerably lower volatility. Hence, ATX and Eurostoxx50 might appear a better option for the risk averse investors. Last but not least, Eurostoxx50 as well as ATX posses lower prices, than prices of other ETFs

Part d

library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
ETFdata <- ETFdata %>% 
  mutate(
    ATX_mr = (ATX - lag(ATX))/lag(ATX),
    DAX_mr = (DAX - lag(DAX))/lag(DAX),
    Eurostoxx50_mr = (Eurostoxx50 - lag(Eurostoxx50))/lag(Eurostoxx50),
    NASDAQ_mr = (Nasdaq.Composite - lag(Nasdaq.Composite))/lag(Nasdaq.Composite),
    Hangseng_mr = (Hangseng - lag(Hangseng))/lag(Hangseng)
  ) #Adding the new columns to the data set, which represent monthly return rates for each ETF separately

Part e

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata[,c(7-11)], aes(x = ATX_mr))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, color = "black", fill = "coral2", linetype = "longdash")+
  theme_dark()
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Plot for the monthly returns of ATX
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata[,c(7-11)], aes(x = DAX_mr))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, color = "black", fill = "goldenrod3", linetype = "longdash")+
  theme_dark()
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Plot for the monthly returns of DAX
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata[,c(7-11)], aes(x = Eurostoxx50_mr))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, color = "black", fill = "darkseagreen3", linetype = "longdash")+
  theme_dark()
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Plot for the monthly returns of Eurostoxx50
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata[,c(7-11)], aes(x = NASDAQ_mr))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, color = "black", fill = "darkorchid", linetype = "longdash")+
  theme_dark()
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Plot for the monthly returns of NASDAQ composite
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(ETFdata[,c(7-11)], aes(x = Hangseng_mr))+
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, color = "black", fill = "deepskyblue1", linetype = "longdash")+
  theme_dark()
## Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
## (`stat_bin()`).

#Plot for the monthly returns of Hangseng

Analysis

Beginning characterization with ATX, we may begin with observation of some return rates’ frequency between nearly -6% and 10% monthly. The highest frequency can be seen between 1% and near 4%, which let us make a conclusion, that the returns aren’t high but at the same time show low risks. As all other securities observed, ATX also possesses some outliers Considering the histogram of Dax, it’s easy to see that it has most of its values between 2% and 3%. The losses are also possible, though they ae not significant Monthly returns of Eurostoxx50 ETF appear to be quite robust, since its biggest part lays around 0%. Such RoR trend can be also explained on the graph of this security’s prices, since the volatility of it is considerably low Nasdaq composite features oftener high returns, in comparison to the biggest part of data. On the other hand, both Hangseng and Nasdaq composite display high risk which might atract some risk-seeking investors
